section.title: Greeting to the Elect
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with a greeting that is packed with theology. In two verses Peter names his readers as chosen exiles and grounds their whole identity in the work of Father, Spirit, and Son. Everything the letter will say about hope, holiness, and suffering flows from who these scattered believers already are in God.
section.thesis: Though they are exiles scattered across Asia Minor and about to suffer for the faith, Peter's readers are the elect of God, chosen by the Father, sanctified by the Spirit, and set apart for obedience to Christ and cleansing by His blood, so grace and peace are theirs in abundance.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the writer and his scattered readers, verse 1; and the triune ground of their election with the greeting, verse 2.

units.label: The apostle and the exiles
units.note: chosen sojourners in a hostile world

verses.text: Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ, To the {{eklektois|elect}} who are {{parepidemois|exiles}} of the {{diaspora|Dispersion}} throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, chosen

units.label: Chosen by Father, Spirit, and Son

verses.text: according to the {{prognosis|foreknowledge}} of God the Father and {{hagiasmos|sanctified by the Spirit}} for obedience to Jesus Christ and {{rhantismos|sprinkling by His blood}}: Grace and peace be yours in abundance.

eklektois.gloss: Chosen, elect, selected by God. Peter's first word for his readers is not their weakness or their scattering but their election. The very people the world treats as outsiders are the ones God has personally chosen, and this dignity anchors the whole letter.

parepidemois.gloss: A resident alien, a temporary sojourner, one living away from home. It pictures believers as strangers passing through a land not their own. Their citizenship is elsewhere, so the hostility they meet in this world fits their status as pilgrims, not a sign that God has forgotten them.

diaspora.gloss: The Dispersion, the scattering of a people away from their homeland. Once used of Jews scattered among the nations, Peter applies it to Christians spread across the provinces. God's chosen people are sown throughout the world, exiles by circumstance yet gathered as one by grace.

prognosis.gloss: Foreknowledge, God's knowing beforehand that carries the sense of choosing and setting His love upon. Election rests on the Father's prior, loving purpose, not on anything the believer first did. It means God's choice of His people reaches back before they were ever aware of Him.

hagiasmos.gloss: Sanctification, being set apart and made holy. Here it is the Spirit's work of consecrating believers to God. Election becomes real in experience as the Spirit sets a person apart, marking out the chosen as those being made holy for the Lord's service.

rhantismos.gloss: Sprinkling, the ritual application of sacrificial blood. It recalls Moses sprinkling the covenant blood on Israel, Exodus 24, now fulfilled in Christ. Believers are brought into the new covenant and cleansed by the sprinkled blood of Jesus, sealing them for obedience to Him.

basic.context: Peter writes to Christians scattered across what is now Turkey, believers who feel like strangers in their own towns and are beginning to suffer for their faith. His very first move is to remind them who they are. They are not forgotten misfits; they are God's chosen people, exiles only in the sense that their true home is with Him. In two short verses he shows that Father, Spirit, and Son are all at work in their salvation.
basic.hermeneutics: Peter states facts about his readers before he asks anything of them. He does not begin with commands but with their identity: chosen, set apart, cleansed. This is the pattern of the whole letter, and of the gospel, gift before demand. When later he calls them to holiness and endurance, it flows out of what God has already made them.
basic.originalLanguages: They are the eklektoi, the chosen, living as parepidemoi, resident aliens, scattered in the diaspora. Their election rests on the prognosis, the loving foreknowledge, of the Father, is worked out by the hagiasmos, the setting apart, of the Spirit, and is sealed by the rhantismos, the sprinkling, of Christ's blood.
basic.typology: The sprinkling of blood points back to Moses sealing the covenant at Sinai by sprinkling blood on the people, Exodus 24. What that pictured, Christ fulfills: His blood brings believers into the new covenant. And the exile language echoes Israel scattered among the nations, now answered in a people gathered by grace.
basic.christ: Everything centres on Jesus Christ. Peter is His apostle, the readers are chosen for obedience to Him, and it is His blood that sprinkles and cleanses them. The whole Trinity works, but the goal is that these exiles belong to Christ and live under His lordship.
basic.god: God is the Father who chooses His people in love before they ever sought Him, the Spirit who sets them apart, and the Son whose blood cleanses them. He is a God who saves by the joint work of all three persons, and who lavishes grace and peace on His scattered, suffering children.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 24 shows the covenant sealed by sprinkled blood. Ephesians 1 speaks of being chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world. Deuteronomy 7 shows God setting His love on a people not because of their strength. And John 17 shows the Father giving a people to the Son.
basic.oneStory: God has always been gathering a chosen people for Himself, from Israel called out of the nations to the church scattered through the provinces. These exiles are the latest chapter of that one story, chosen by the Father, sanctified by the Spirit, and sealed by the blood of Christ, on their way home.

context.literary: This is the letter's salutation, following the ancient form of writer, readers, and greeting, but Peter fills each part with gospel weight. The naming of five Roman provinces traces a rough circuit through Asia Minor, likely the route the letter carrier would travel. The dense description of the readers in verses 1 to 2 sets the themes of election, holiness, obedience, and suffering that the whole letter develops.
context.historical: Peter writes, most take it, from Rome, called Babylon in 5:13, in the early to mid 60s, to mixed congregations of Jewish and Gentile believers in the northern and central provinces of Asia Minor. These Christians were a social minority, viewed with suspicion, facing slander and growing pressure. The exile language names their real experience of not belonging.
context.canonical: The greeting joins the Bible's long story of election and covenant. The language of chosen people, dispersion, and sprinkled blood deliberately draws Old Testament Israel's vocabulary onto the church. It also stands among the New Testament letters as an example of how apostolic greetings compress rich theology into a few opening lines.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To identify Peter as Christ's apostle, to name his scattered readers by their true identity as God's chosen exiles, and to ground that identity in the saving work of Father, Spirit, and Son before asking anything of them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These verses are almost entirely description: they tell believers who they are and what God has done. There is no command here, only the pronouncement of grace and peace. The prescriptions of the letter, be holy, submit, endure, all rest on this prior description of identity.

debates.question: In what sense are the readers exiles and of the Dispersion?

views.label: Primarily spiritual and figurative
views.case: The whole letter treats believers as strangers whose citizenship is in heaven; the exile is their status as pilgrims in a world that is not their home, whatever their ethnic background.

views.label: Also socially and literally true
views.case: These were real people genuinely displaced and marginalized in their provinces, some perhaps Jewish believers of the actual Dispersion, so the term names both a social reality and a spiritual truth.

debates.bearing: Either way the point holds: God's chosen live as sojourners in this age. The pilgrim identity shapes how they face suffering and hold their possessions loosely, longing for their true home.

debates.question: What does chosen according to the foreknowledge of God mean?

views.label: Foreknowledge as God's sovereign, loving choice
views.case: In Scripture God's knowing of His people is covenantal and elective, setting His love on them beforehand. Election rests on the Father's gracious purpose, not on foreseen merit or faith as its cause.

views.label: Foreknowledge as God foreseeing a response
views.case: Others read it as God knowing in advance who would believe. Even so, Peter's stress falls on God's initiative and the security it gives; the chosen are held by the Father's prior knowledge of them.

debates.bearing: Both orthodox readings assure suffering believers that their standing rests on God's eternal purpose, not their own strength. Election is meant here as comfort and dignity, not cold speculation.

study.originalLanguages: Peter addresses the eklektoi, the chosen, who live as parepidemoi, resident aliens, in the diaspora, the scattering. Their election flows from the prognosis of the Father, His loving foreknowledge that sets His purpose on them before they sought Him. It is worked out by the hagiasmos of the Spirit, the setting apart that makes them holy, and it is sealed by the rhantismos of Christ's blood, the sprinkling that brings them into the covenant and cleanses them for obedience. In one dense sentence the salvation of these exiles is shown to be the work of Father, Spirit, and Son together.

typology.fulfillment: The covenant sealed at Sinai by Moses sprinkling blood on the people, Exodus 24:8, fulfilled in the blood of Christ that brings believers into the new covenant and cleanses them.
Israel scattered among the nations, now answered in the church gathered from every province and people as one chosen family in Christ.

study.christ: Jesus Christ frames the whole greeting. Peter writes as His apostle, sent by His authority. The readers are chosen for obedience to Him, brought under His lordship. And they are sealed by the sprinkling of His blood, the sacrifice that cleanses and covenants them to God. The Father chooses and the Spirit sanctifies, but the movement is all toward belonging to Christ, obeying Him and being made clean by His shed blood.
study.god: God is presented as one and yet as Father, Spirit, and Son together working salvation. The Father chooses His people according to His own foreknowledge, setting His love on them before they ever turned to Him. The Spirit sanctifies, setting them apart as holy. The Son sheds His blood to cleanse and claim them. This is a generous, saving God who lavishes grace and peace in abundance on exiles who might otherwise feel abandoned.

crossReferences.note: Moses seals the covenant by sprinkling blood on the people, the picture Peter says is fulfilled in the sprinkled blood of Christ.
God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world, matching Peter's stress on election by the Father's foreknowledge.
God sets His love on a people and chooses them, not for their strength, the pattern behind calling these exiles the elect.
The Father gives a people to the Son, the triune shape of election that Peter compresses into his greeting.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has been gathering a chosen people for Himself, calling Abraham, redeeming Israel, sealing them in covenant blood. When His people were scattered among the nations, the promise did not fail; it widened. Now the church, spread through the provinces as exiles, is that chosen people, selected by the Father, made holy by the Spirit, and cleansed by the blood of the Son. The greeting sets these strangers within the one great story that runs from election to homecoming.

quiz.q: What is the first thing Peter calls his scattered readers?

options.text: The elect, God's chosen people.
options.note: Verse 1: before naming their suffering, Peter names their election.

options.text: Exiles of the Dispersion.
options.note: True, and vivid, but their exile is framed by the deeper truth that they are chosen.

options.text: Weak believers who need correction.
options.note: Peter opens with dignity and grace, not rebuke.

options.text: Citizens fully at home in their cities.
options.note: The opposite: they are sojourners whose true home is with God.

quiz.q: How does Peter describe the basis of the readers' election?

options.text: The foreknowledge of God the Father, His loving choice set on them beforehand.
options.note: Verse 2: election rests on the Father's prior purpose.

options.text: God foreseeing that they would one day believe.
options.note: A held view, though Peter's stress falls on God's own initiative and the security it gives.

options.text: Their faithful obedience earning God's favor.
options.note: Obedience is the goal of election here, not its ground.

options.text: Their membership in a particular nation by birth.
options.note: The chosen are gathered from many provinces by grace, not by ancestry.

quiz.q: Which persons of the Godhead are at work in these two verses?

options.text: Father, Spirit, and Son all together in the readers' salvation.
options.note: Verse 2: chosen by the Father, sanctified by the Spirit, sprinkled by the blood of the Son.

options.text: The Father and the Son, with the Spirit implied.
options.note: Close, but the Spirit is named explicitly as the one who sanctifies.

options.text: Only God the Father, acting alone.
options.note: The Father chooses, but the Spirit and Son are named as well.

options.text: Only Jesus Christ, without the Father or Spirit.
options.note: All three persons appear; the work is triune.

quiz.q: What does the sprinkling by His blood recall from the Old Testament?

options.text: Moses sealing the covenant by sprinkling blood on the people at Sinai.
options.note: Exodus 24:8, fulfilled in the covenant blood of Christ that cleanses believers.

options.text: The Passover lamb's blood on the doorposts.
options.note: A related sacrificial picture, though the sprinkling here points chiefly to the covenant at Sinai.

options.text: The washing rituals of ordinary daily life.
options.note: This is covenant and sacrificial blood, not ordinary washing.

options.text: Nothing; it is only a figure of speech.
options.note: It draws directly on the covenant sacrifices of Israel, now fulfilled in Christ.

quiz.q: Why does Peter begin with the readers' identity before any command?

options.text: Because gospel identity comes first, and obedience flows out of who God has made us.
options.note: Grace before demand is the pattern of the whole letter.

options.text: Because he wants to encourage them before asking hard things of them.
options.note: True pastorally, though the deeper reason is the gospel order of gift then response.

options.text: Because their conduct does not really matter to him.
options.note: Conduct matters greatly; it simply rests on identity, which comes first.

options.text: Because he had nothing to ask of them.
options.note: The letter is full of exhortation; it is grounded here in identity.

questions: Peter calls suffering, scattered believers God's elect. How would it change your week to see yourself first as chosen by God rather than by your circumstances?
The readers are exiles whose true home is with God. Where are you tempted to settle down as if this world were your final home?
Salvation here is the work of Father, Spirit, and Son together. How does knowing the whole Trinity is committed to your rescue steady you in hard times?
Election rests on God's loving foreknowledge, not your performance. What difference does it make to know your standing was secured before you ever sought Him?
Peter wishes grace and peace in abundance. Where do you most need God's abundant grace and peace to meet you right now?

translationNotes.note: The elect who are exiles of the Dispersion renders a phrase that literally reads the elect sojourners of the Diaspora; the provinces named lie in what is now Turkey.
Foreknowledge translates prognosis, which in Scripture carries the sense of God's loving, elective choice beforehand, not merely knowing facts in advance.
Sprinkling by His blood echoes the covenant sealing of Exodus 24, applying the sacrificial cleansing of Christ to the believer.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 1:1-2. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
